title: Run your first program
instruction:
Here it is — your very first Go program. You don't need to change anything yet. Just hit Run and watch what happens.

Look at the output panel. Do you see "Hello, World!"? That's your computer following the instruction you gave it. One line of text, exactly as requested.
hint: No changes needed at all! Just press the Run button and watch the output appear.

title: Make it say your name
instruction:
Now let's make it personal. Change the message so the program says hello to you.

If your name is Jordan, it should print: Hello, Jordan!

Everything between the quote marks is what gets printed. That's the only thing you need to change.
hint: Find the part that says "Hello, World!" and replace World with your own name. Keep the quote marks on both sides.

title: Print two lines
instruction:
Each time you write fmt.Println, it prints one line and then moves to the next line automatically. Like pressing Enter after you type something.

Add a second fmt.Println below the first one. Make the first line say your name, and the second line say your hometown. They should print one after the other.
hint: Copy the fmt.Println line and paste it right below. Then change the text inside the quotes for each one. Two lines of code, two lines of output.

title: Introduce yourself
instruction:
Let's print a proper 3-line introduction. Print these three things, each on its own line:

Your name
Your age
One fun fact about yourself

You'll need three separate fmt.Println calls — one for each line.
hint: You need three lines of code, each starting with fmt.Println. Put a different piece of information in each one. Something like: fmt.Println("My name is Sam"), fmt.Println("I am 24"), fmt.Println("I can juggle").

title: Leave a comment
instruction:
Comments are notes you leave in your code for yourself or other people. The computer ignores them completely — they're just for humans.

A comment starts with two forward slashes: //

Add a comment above each fmt.Println explaining what it prints. The program should still produce the same output — comments don't affect what runs.
hint: A comment looks like this: // This line prints my name. Place it on the line directly above the Println it describes. You should end up with three comments and three Println calls.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	fmt.Println("My name is Alex")
	fmt.Println("I am 22 years old")
	fmt.Println("I enjoy hiking")
}

codeChecks.message: Add at least one comment — a line that starts with //

title: Fix the broken program
instruction:
Something is wrong with this program. It won't run. Your job is to figure out what's broken and fix it.

This is an important skill — reading error messages and finding bugs. Every programmer does this constantly. Don't panic when you see an error. Read it, look at your code, and find the mismatch.

Hint: Go is very picky about quote marks and capitalization.
hint: Look carefully at the Println calls. One of them has a problem with the quote marks, and one has a capitalization issue. Fix both and the program will run.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	fmt.Println("This line is fine")
	fmt.println("this line has a capital letter problem")
	fmt.Println("this line has a missing quote)
}
expectedOutput: this line has a capital letter problem
this line has a missing quote
